spec_match.rationale: Two tagged-template helpers (`re` and `likePattern`) feed every interpolation / literal byte through `RegExp.escape`, which is the canonical user-input-safe pattern the proposal motivates.

interactive.rationale: Two editable panels now each render safe and raw outputs side by side, with live pattern strings, match counts, and highlights on every keystroke.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature index promises `re` escaping interpolations and `likePattern` preserving SQL-LIKE wildcards while escaping literal bytes; the side-by-side outputs now make both claims visible.

edge_cases.rationale: Shows raw-vs-safe interpolation failure and a LIKE wildcard case where unescaped punctuation overmatches; a newline/tab/surrogate escape inspection remains unshown.

references.rationale: The see-also list now includes the required ChromeStatus feature link and the TC39 proposal link, alongside sibling concepts.

design_system.rationale: Inline styles use CSS variables only, no raw hex, no button overrides — clean.

openQuestions.title: Cover the spec's hex-escape edge case in `likePattern`
openQuestions.detail: Add an example where the literal text includes a newline, tab, or surrogate pair — the spec mandates `\xHH`/`\uHHHH` escapes for those, but a one-char `ch === '%' ? '.*' : RegExp.escape(ch)` loop will quietly emit the correct output without flagging it. A debug strip showing the per-char escape output would surface this.

summary: Resolved the major and moderate issues: the page now cites ChromeStatus and TC39, shows `re` versus raw interpolation side by side, and uses a real LIKE wildcard pattern where `%` and `_` match the intended log lines while unsafe literal handling overmatches punctuation.
